pattern: Because at most two removals can always separate any connected shape on a grid, the answer only ever takes one of three values -- so check whether it is already disconnected, then whether any single cell's removal disconnects it, and fall back to the remaining case.
hints: First count connected land regions with a flood fill: if that count is already anything other than exactly one, zero days are needed.

If it's a single connected region, try removing each land cell one at a time (turning it to water), re-run the flood fill, and check whether that one change alone breaks it into zero or multiple regions.
If no single removal works, the answer is always two -- a connected shape on a grid never needs more than two cell removals to separate it, so that is the guaranteed fallback.

edge_inputs.description: a single land cell, already its own island with no way to disconnect further by removal
an all-water grid, already disconnected with zero islands
a solid 2x2 block of land, where removing any single corner still leaves a connected L-shape
a straight line of three land cells, where removing the middle cell splits it into two islands
two separate land cells already forming two islands, needing no removal at all
a plus-shaped island where the center cell is a single point of failure
a single row with only two adjacent land cells, needing both removed to reach zero islands
a thin ring of land surrounding a water hole, where no single cell removal breaks the loop

stress.description: random small grids with a random land density, sized within the constraint limits
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 10)))
    n = rng.randint(1, max(1, min(size, 10)))
    density = rng.uniform(0.3, 0.8)
    grid = [[1 if rng.random() < density else 0 for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]
    return [grid]
stress.note: kept small (up to 10x10) since the reference solution re-runs a flood fill for every land cell

complexity.time: O((m*n)^2) -- a flood fill of the whole grid retried for every land cell
